37	## Conclusions and Research Gaps
38	
39	Early research findings focusing on how AI affects labor demand are collectively inconsistent, and any findings based on current data are necessarily weak signals about the future.[82] Multiple studies using the Current Population Survey—including work by Gimbel et al. (2025), Eckhardt and Goldschlag (2025), and Dominski and Lee (2025)—show at most small changes in hiring in AI-exposed jobs.[84] Separately, Frank et al. (2026) found similar results, and some analyses argue that the timing of declines in job postings corresponds better to rising interest rates than to the launch of large language models.[82] Despite this uncertainty, one source reports that AI exposure measures from Eloundou et al. (2024) have very high correlation with Microsoft Copilot usage from 2025, suggesting that earlier predictions were largely borne out.[84] However, existing measures of AI exposure are still criticized for lacking validation on real-world economic outcomes, and the mismatch between supply and demand for research on this topic is described as unlike anything previously seen.[84] The most experienced and highest-skilled workers saw minimal or even slightly negative effects, suggesting that generative AI functions as a skill-leveling technology rather than a skill-amplifying one.[85] AI compressed the learning curve substantially, with agents having two months of experience performing like agents with six months without AI.[85] Separately, gains from AI adoption were found to be largest for moderately rare problems, where human agents have less baseline experience but the system still has adequate training data.[86] The market structure of AI itself may shape these outcomes, as the two top-ranked foundation model systems differ by only 37 points on the LMSYS scale, translating to approximately a 55.3% chance of one outperforming the other, indicating that frontier models are close substitutes.[87] One analysis characterizes AI's impact on work thus far as "routine-biased technological deployment on steroids," and studies on the impact of machine learning–based AI conclude that, as in prior eras of automation, jobs remain in spite of these shifts.[88] (also mirrored) Whether GenAI will support a continuation of the polarization of work, which analysts widely agree has been underway, or whether it will open possibilities for the augmentation of worker opportunities and the creation of new categories of high-quality jobs, remains an open question.[88] (also mirrored) AI also has unclear effects on matching between employers and job candidates: algorithmic writing assistance for resumes leads to causal increases in hiring and wages for prospective employees, and AI-assisted interviewing leads to better candidate selection.[84] On the other hand, AI usage improves cover letters but makes them less informative signals of worker ability, prompting employers to shift toward alternative signals such as past reviews, and if all resumes begin to look the same, it may become harder to form good matches.[84] The growing integration of AI into academic research workflows itself poses challenges, as AI-generated literature reviews are increasingly being rejected for lacking genuine academic analysis, with journals tightening standards around AI-assisted writing.[91] A systematic review of AI applications in academic writing identified thematic approaches including possible applications, specific tools, plagiarism detection, and ethical use, underscoring the need for further investigation of how AI can improve clarity and accuracy in scientific communication.[92] The evidence on how AI is affecting the labor market today is inconclusive, and claims about harmful impacts on particular groups of workers are premature.[82] One pessimistic estimate from Acemoglu (2024) suggests that the growth effects of generative AI will be only 0.07% per year over the coming decade, which, if correct, would render the market concentration implications a footnote in economic history.[87] The German debate over the potential of "Industry 4.0" predominantly hinges on different views about who controls the production process and how the tools are used.[88] (also mirrored) Platform firms have become a crucial part of the market economy and GenAI may drive significant changes in the organization of work and in market competition.[88] (also mirrored) A study measures the impacts of labor in the platform economy in terms of new work created, old work reorganized, and value creation reconfigured.[88] (also mirrored) The available evidence on AI's labor market effects remains nascent and uncertain, with research having barely scratched the surface.[84][82] The safe deployment of AI is identified as an essential question, with implications for social and economic well-being as well as the ultimate risk to human existence from AI.[88] (also mirrored) In sum, the evidence suggests that AI is beginning to have measurable effects on productivity, particularly for entry-level and lower-skilled workers, but important questions about AI's broader effects on the labor market remain unanswered[84][82][85]

under full automation of exposed tasks, with GDP impact remaining modest at approximately 1.1%.[93] IMF simulations by Cerutti et al. illustrate two contrasting trajectories: a rapid adoption scenario in which global TFP grows by 2.4% over ten years and global GDP increases by 4%, versus a slower scenario with TFP growth of only 1.8% and GDP growth limited to approximately 1.3%.[93] Census Bureau study reported that only 5.4% of firms were using AI technologies in spring 2024, up from 3.7% the year prior.[94] A separate analysis by Bonney et al. documents that as of February 2024, only 5.4% of 2.8% of work hours, which contrasts with productivity gains exceeding 15% documented by randomized controlled trials in the same occupations.[95] A study published in Science found that generative AI can boost output by as much as 40% in text-based tasks, but novices who accept machine suggestions uncritically perform worse than those who reason through problems themselves.[97] Research on job transformation develops a general-equilibrium model in which moderate AI exposure benefits workers on average but high exposure harms them, with the return to social skills rising and the return to analytical skills falling.[98] Gmyrek et al. estimate that only 3.3% of jobs will be exposed to full automation, a figure that rises to 7% in the analysis by Chui et al., reinforcing the consensus report feeling pressure to implement AI.[99] The global AI customer service market is projected at $15.12 billion in 2026, growing at a 25.8% compound annual rate toward $47.82 billion by 2030.[99] 117.72 per 1,000 in automotive production.[100] The WEF reports that global average robot density reached 162 units per 10,000 employees in 2023, double the number measured seven years prior, with 80% of installations concentrated in China, Japan, and the United States.[101] AI-driven recruitment tools have also raised fairness concerns, with scholars noting that AI applications in hiring can amplify and introduce new forms of intersectional discrimination given datasets structured along axes of social inequality.[102] The analysis of Industry 4.0 adoption in supply chain operations found 525 papers related to Industry 4.0 broadly, but only 20 papers specifically addressed supply chain dimensions, highlighting a significant research gap.[103] The Future of Jobs Report finds that 86% of respondents expect AI and information processing technologies to transform their business by 2030, positioning AI as the technology with the single largest anticipated impact.[101] A large-scale study of AI chatbot adoption across 11 exposed occupations in Denmark, linked to administrative data, estimates precise null effects on earnings and recorded hours at both the individual and workplace levels, with confidence intervals ruling out effects above 1%.[95] Nearly all respondents reported that AI chatbots have had no impact on their earnings.[95] That same study observed a 3.7-minute decrease in average handle time, representing an 8.5% decline from a baseline of 43 minutes.[104] Morgan reports that the unemployment rate among college graduates reached 5.8% in March, the highest in more than four years.[105]

No contradictions were detected by the pipeline, so no cross-source disagreement on magnitude, direction, or endpoint is asserted. The telemetry block does not surface an explicit date range for the corpus, which limits the ability to characterize the evidence horizon or identify temporal gaps in source coverage. These distributional and provenance constraints mean the pipeline's synthesis capacity is weighted toward interpretation-layer material rather than original experimental or observational data.

59:Artificial intelligence primarily functions as a cost-reduction mechanism for prediction, thereby altering the economics of decision-making tasks. Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. report that understanding the impact of AI on jobs requires understanding the relative roles of prediction and decision tasks. Furthermore, Agrawal et al., writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, show that AI affects labor differently depending on whether the automation of prediction automates decisions altogether or enhances human decision-making. This establishes a theoretical foundation distinguishing between full task automation and human augmentation. **[Established finding]**
131:While individual firms benefit from automation, broader regional labor markets suffer. Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al. However, at the regional level, the adoption of industrial robotics negatively impacts regional employment and wages. Writing in the Journal of Organization Design in 2019, Raj and Seamans show this negative outcome. The evidence cannot distinguish whether these contrasting outcomes stem from localized displacement effects or a mismatch between firm-level productivity gains and regional labor demand. **[Contested]**
133:Writing in the Journal of Organization Design in 2019, Raj and Seamans show that industrial robots lead to manufacturing job losses, but these are offset by new roles in the service industry. This economy-wide effect aligns with manufacturing-specific firm outcomes. Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al. These findings jointly establish that manufacturing bears the brunt of automation shocks while service sectors absorb the displaced labor or productivity gains. **[Established finding]**
141:Writing in the The Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2002, Bresnahan et al. show that the combination of IT, workplace reorganization, and new products/services constitutes a skill-biased technical change that shifts labor demand toward more skilled workers. This long-run economy-wide shift toward skilled labor aligns with firm-level productivity gains in specific sectors. Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al. **[Established finding]**
---orphan tails (sentence starting lowercase show/report)---
17:Artificial intelligence fundamentally alters firm-level operations by necessitating organizational restructuring rather than serving as a standalone technological plug-in. Writing in the Business Process Management Journal in 2020, Wamba-Taguimdje et al. show that organizations only achieve performance gains from AI capabilities if they use these technologies to reconfigure their processes. This aligns with broader technological shifts, as Bresnahan et al., writing in the The Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2002, report that IT affects labor demand not only directly but indirectly through firm-level organizational changes and product innovation. **[Established finding]** However, the evidence does not establish whether these changes fully close the gap between AI's theoretical promise and its practical execution, as Writing in the California Management Review in 2019, Tambe et al. show that a significant gap exists between the expected promise and actual reality of AI applications in human resource management.
21:The broader restructuring of labor markets extends beyond the firm boundary, manifesting as long-run industry ecosystem disruption and macroeconomic wage polarization. Writing in the Business Process Management Journal in 2020, Wamba-Taguimdje et al. show that organizations are adopting AI innovations to adapt to or disrupt their ecosystems and optimize competitive advantages. This industry-level disruption parallels historical macroeconomic trends, as Bresnahan et al., writing in the The Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2002, report that the shift toward more skilled workers accelerated from 1980 to the mid-1990s, substantially shifting relative wages across the income distribution. Furthermore, while Wamba-Taguimdje et al., writing in the Business Process Management Journal in 2020, report that AI technologies like machine translation and chatbots allow individuals to better understand their environment and act accordingly, the aggregate economic implications of this worker-level empowerment remain unresolved. **[Unresolved]**
35:Writing in the California Management Review in 2019, Shrestha et al. show that AI-based and human decision-making differ along five key contingency factors: search space specificity, interpretability, alternative set size, speed, and replicability. **[Established finding]**
37:The technical feasibility of automation alone cannot drive large-scale workforce substitution, as broader economic constraints ultimately dictate the pace of restructuring. Writing in the Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research in 2021, Tolan et al. show that the technical feasibility of automating a task is not sufficient for large-scale labor substitution, as economic factors like labor cost and demand elasticity also matter. By analyzing the occupation level, this research clarifies that task automation and labor substitution are not synonymous phenomena. **[Unresolved]**
43:Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2015, Autor shows that computers substitute for workers in routine tasks while amplifying the human comparative advantage in problem-solving, adaptability, and creativity. Writing in the The Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2002, Bresnahan et al. report that computer automation primarily substitutes for routine clerical and similar tasks but has limited scope for automating complex managerial and professional work. **[Established finding]** Similarly, writing in the Human Resource Management Review in 2023, Chowdhury et al. note that automated intelligence allows human workers to spend more time on non-trivial complex tasks by automating routine and manual tasks.
47:The precise realization of these displacement effects depends heavily on organizational and economic contexts rather than technical feasibility alone. Writing in the The Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2002, Bresnahan et al. show that the effects of IT on labor demand are larger when IT is combined with specific organizational investments. input.diagnosis: Turn 2 bolted one confirmed lever (+6.0 journal attribution) onto output machinery that was actively mutilating three dimensions, and shipped it without a single output-level lint. The specific kill chain: (1) THE SPLITTER AMPUTATES AT "et al." — cellcog_composer.py:391 `re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', p)` cuts every "Agrawal et al. report that X" into a head ("...Agrawal et al.") and a tail ("report that X"); proven by direct execution. The head passes the attribution regex and ships as a complete 'sentence'; the tail fails it, is misclassified as synthesis, and is either gate-dropped (74 drops) or shipped as a lowercase orphan. The turn-2 report contains 5 dangling "Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al." full-stop stumps, adjacent duplicated heads (line 59), orphan "show that..." fragments (line 121), and "**[Unresolved]**" as an ENTIRE subsection body (line 177). The judge quoted these amputations back verbatim: "duplicated clauses, incomplete sentences, missing words". (2) THE GATE IS A RACE CONDITION — `_SYNTH_CARDS` is a module global (:324) reassigned by 6 threads (:434-435, ThreadPoolExecutor at :441), so sentences were validated against a RANDOM other subsection's premises; drops were nondeterministic, whole subsections evaporated. (3) 27 STATELESS PARALLEL WRITERS + overlapping greedy `_select` re-narrated the same card up to 5x ("technical feasibility is not sufficient" 5x, "static model with fixed capital" 5x, Bresnahan QJE attribution 10x) — roughly 1,500-2,000 of 6,305 words are restatement, putting UNIQUE content near ~4.5k words, BELOW the measured 5,000w floor, with zero transitions because no writer ever saw neighboring text: that is the judge's "fragmented narrative... without adequate transitions", and it is why paragraph-size/H3 surgery moved readability -0.08 — we shortened the fragments and left them disconnected. (4) ONE MANDATED TEMPLATE (WRITE_PROMPT :295-298) stamped 135 times, plus "use this exact wording" propagating "the The Quarterly Journal of Economics" 15x — the judge's #1 readability complaint names "Writing in" explicitly. (5) THE 4IR SECTIONS DIED AT EXTRACTION, NOT IN THE CORPUS — 7 of the 70 corpus papers discuss 4IR/Industry 4.0, but EXTRACT_PROMPT (:102) asks only for labor-market FINDINGS, so 0 of 133 cards mention 4IR; `_select` then fed the '4IR differs from previous three' subsection cards about researcher attention-shifting (top lexical score: 2), and both 4IR subsections plus 5 others (7/28) silently vanished via `results.get(...,'')`. 4IR-named criteria scored 4.5/4.2/5.0 across all three content dimensions. (6) We DELETED the bibliography instead of testing a cleaner-proof format; the judge penalized its absence inside our one winning criterion ("lacks a formal reference list, making verification difficult" — capped at 7.5). Net arithmetic: +0.63 on one dimension against -0.58/-1.14/-0.08 on three. The lever was real — we beat the reference on journal-only (7.5 vs 4.0), English-only (9.0 vs 8.0), AND literature depth (7.0 vs 6.8) — but everything around it was an unlinted regression, and changing 5 things at once meant only the judge's written receipt made it diagnosable.

input.corpus_decision: COMPOSE from the journal corpus — but expand it by harvesting the journal-tier subset of rank10's sources, and treat rank10 as a SOURCE MAP, never as text to transform. The -1.14 collapse looks like an indictment of the corpus swap, but the per-criterion decomposition says otherwise: within comprehensiveness we BEAT the reference on Depth/Representativeness of Literature (7.0 vs 6.8) — the canonical-journal spine is a strength — while the collapse concentrated in Industry Scope (5.2 vs ref 8.0), Disruption Scale (5.5 vs 8.3), and 4IR Grounding (4.5 vs 8.5). Tracing each: the 4IR hole is an EXTRACTION bug (7 corpus papers discuss 4IR; 0 cards were extracted because the prompt only asked for labor-market findings); a further chunk is the 7/28 subsections that silently evaporated in the gate/race; and ~1,500-2,000 words of the 6,305 were duplicate restatements, leaving unique content near ~4.5k — under the measured 5,000w comprehensiveness floor. So roughly half of the -1.14 is recoverable by fixing machinery alone, no corpus change needed. The remainder (industry breadth, scale numbers) needs sources — and rank10's own reference list contains ~15-20 genuine journal-tier items (QJE, Science, SMJ, JEP, JSM, ETP) that cover exactly those gaps; add them to the corpus and re-extract. TRANSFORMING the rank10 report itself fails three ways: (1) its breadth is load-bearing on unciteable sources (Toptal, substack, HR Executive, WEF blogs) — visible attribution of those re-creates the 1.5/10 citation score we just escaped, and re-attributing their facts to journals they didn't come from is fabrication, which this pipeline exists to prevent; (2) its text is rubric poison that needs full rewrite anyway — it opens with meta-commentary ('This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above') and ends with a telemetry 'Limitations' section, both on the composer's own banned list, in 677-word paragraphs with 240 doomed [n] markers; (3) a full rewrite of 97-source text WITHOUT span-grounding reintroduces unverifiable-claim risk everywhere. The card pipeline is the faithfulness machinery and the attribution lever's substrate; keep it, feed it more journal papers, and fix the two stages (extraction contract, output gate) that actually destroyed comprehensiveness. Bonus: since R is not fixed (8.03 vs us, 7.36 vs cellcog), every recovered comprehensiveness point pays twice.
input.prose_defect_root_cause: ARCHITECTURE, with a prompt accessory and token budget a distant, real third. Primary — three code-level bugs in /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/cellcog_composer.py: (1) the sentence splitter `re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', p)` at :391 splits at every 'et al. ' — proven by direct execution ('...Agrawal et al.' | 'report that...') — and the downstream attribution test at :403 then misroutes the tail into the synthesis gate, which drops it (74 drops) or ships it as a lowercase orphan; the dangling head ships either way. Every defect the judge listed — 'duplicated clauses' (line 59's doubled head), 'incomplete sentences' (5x 'Damioli et al.' full-stop stumps at lines 131/133/141), 'missing words' (line 121's 'show that...' orphans), 'abrupt endings' (line 177's body = '**[Unresolved]**') — is mechanically this bug's signature, visible in the artifact. (2) `_SYNTH_CARDS` is a module global (:324) written by 6 concurrent threads (:434-435 under ThreadPoolExecutor :441), so the gate validated sentences against arbitrary other subsections' premises — nondeterministic amputation, emptied subsections. (3) Stateless parallel writers with overlapping greedy `_select` (:333) and no shared ledger guarantee both the repetition (same clause narrated up to 5x, same attribution line 10x) and the zero transitions ('fragmented narrative') — no writer ever saw a neighboring paragraph, so cohesion was architecturally impossible, which is precisely why the structure-only surgery (677w->106w paragraphs, 21 H3s) moved readability -0.08. Secondary — PROMPT: WRITE_PROMPT :295-298 mandates ONE exact attribution shape ('Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>...'), stamped 135 times, plus 'use this exact wording' propagating 'the The Quarterly Journal of Economics' 15x; that is the judge's named phrase-repetition complaint, fixable in the prompt alone. Minor — TOKEN BUDGET: one paragraph ends mid-sentence ('...requires fundamental process and', line 189), a generation-truncation signature the gate cannot produce (it only removes whole splitter-delimited sentences); max_tokens=3000 with glm-5.2 needs a finish_reason check and headroom, but fixing budget alone would leave every other defect intact. The proof of allocation: the judge's complaint list maps item-for-item onto gate artifacts, not onto anything the model was asked and failed to do.
cheap_tests: Splitter unit test (fix 1): feed 'Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. report that X.' through the new splitter — must return exactly 1 sentence; regression-run the 8 known amputation sites from turn 2 (report.md lines 59, 101, 121, 131, 133, 141, 171, 189). Runs in milliseconds, no LLM.

Stump/orphan lint on the assembled report (fix 1, build-fails on any hit): `grep -E 'et al\.\s+(However|These|Writing|\*\*\[|$)'` -> 0; `grep -E '\.\s+(show|report|find|note)s? that'` -> 0; `grep -c 'the The '` -> 0; no paragraph consisting solely of an epistemic label; every subsection's last character in .!?

Determinism test (fix 1, race): compose twice with cached/fixture LLM responses — the dropped-sentence lists must be byte-identical (impossible with the global; today's code fails this by construction). Code-level fallback: `grep -n 'global _SYNTH_CARDS' scripts/cellcog_composer.py` must return nothing.

Template histogram (fix 2): count each attribution shape per subsection (max 2 of any one shape), total 'Writing in' <= 25 document-wide, and no 12-gram shared across 2+ subsections — this catches both the 135-stamp monoculture and the 5x-restatement repetition with one python script, no LLM call.

Cleaner-survival re-run (fixes 2+3): scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py on the 6-8 new attribution shapes AND the new marker-free '## References' block through the PRODUCTION RACE cleaner — require journal-name survival >=10/12 per shape and the references section intact. This is the exact harness that measured 0/12 vs 10-12/12; ~1 minute of LLM cost versus a wasted compose+judge cycle.

Completeness gate (fixes 1+8): all 28 OUTLINE subsections present in the md, each >=120 words; every LLM response's finish_reason == 'stop' (assert in llm(), retry on 'length'); Implications+Conclusion >= 600 words combined.

4IR coverage probe (fixes 4+5): after re-extraction, assert >=8 evidence cards whose claim/span mentions 4IR/Industry 4.0/cyber-physical (currently 0 of 133); on the report, >=12 such mentions spread across >=5 H2 sections plus >=1 explicit contrast with prior industrial revolutions (grep 'steam|electricit|third industrial'). Card-level check runs before any composing.

Breadth proxy (fixes 5+6): distinct journal names in prose >= 25; each sectoral H3 contains its own sector terms (healthcare section mentions health/hospital, etc.); unique-content word count after 12-gram dedup >= 7,000 (turn 2's raw 6,305 concealed ~4.5k unique — below the 5,000w floor); card-usage ledger asserts every card narrated at most once.
